Adventure 6. The Right Stuff. Are you the box man? My young female colleague asked, approaching the table where Tom and I were sitting. I suppose I am, Tom replied graciously. I quickly introduced them. Tom, this is Linda. She handles all of our advertising. Linda, this is Tom, the inventor I told you about. Tom stood up and shook her hand firmly. I hear you've been helping our boss come up with some amazing ideas to keep the bookstore going, said Linda, pulling up a chair from a nearby table. He's the one who's been doing all the heavy lifting. Tom corrected her with a friendly smile. I've just been showing him a new way to get his brain going. Well, I sure could use your help getting mine going. I've heard about some of the idea boxes you've been teaching our boss. Now, I'm supposed to come up with an idea for this big sale we're having and I can't seem to think of anything unique. That sounds like a fun one, said Tom, with far more enthusiasm than I'm usually able to muster for such a task. What have you got so far? Linda sheepishly showed him a half page ad with the headline, Huge Book Sale, followed by the date and all the usual information. That certainly says what they need to know. Tom nodded slowly. It's what we always use, she said with a shrug. It's so...expected. I know it can be better. I'm just not sure where to take it. Well, let's take a look in some boxes, said Tom. I do a little advertising myself when I market some of my own inventions. I usually start out in the Change the Interactions intensity box, since advertising is, after all, an interaction between the advertiser and the prospect. In engineering, we change the intensity by adding or removing elements or by changing the environment. On a grill, pouring gasoline on a fire certainly changes the intensity of the interaction between the charcoal and the fire, doesn't it? Then closing the lid changes the environment by removing oxygen. This changes the interaction in the opposite way. Once the city's Department of Water Works called me because they wanted to speed up the water purification process. They were using bacteria to clean the water, and I was able to speed things up by improving the interaction between the bacteria and the water. We couldn't increase the number or type of bacteria, and we couldn't use bigger tanks. Care to guess how we did it? Oxygen? Came Linda's high-pitched guess. Right! Tom pointed at her excitedly. You got it! Pumping air through the water energized those bacteria and gave them quite an appetite. How did you guess? I took some biology courses in college, she replied sheepishly. Great! Then let's put that scientific mind to work on your advertising, said Tom. Can you think of a way to change the interactivity of your ad to make it more interactive and engaging? Linda thought for a moment. What about a question? That's interactive and engaging. Maybe something like, do you like to buy books, but dislike high prices? Good, said Tom. That might get my attention. Now, can we throw a little more gasoline on the fire and increase the interaction even further? Some emotion, perhaps. I couldn't resist. I blurted out, what about, do you love to buy books, but hate to pay high prices? Good, said Tom quickly. There's nothing like some emotion-laden words to increase the intensity. Any other ways to add fuel to the fire? Linda suggested, what about a warning? That would stir things up. Something like, don't buy another book until you read this ad. Good, said Tom again. You can also use surprise, curiosity, flattery, anything that will increase that interaction between you and the reader. Now, instead of adding fuel, why not try changing the interaction by making it less intense? In advertising today, it seems to me that there's so much shouting going on that sometimes going in the opposite direction and calming things down can really stand out. Kind of like how kindergarten teachers use a soft voice instead of screaming to get their pupils' attention. Linda scrunched up her face and thought for a while, what about just calmly saying something like, a very special invitation to every area book lover who would like to read more and pay less? Wow, I said. That's so understated yet powerful at the same time. Ready for the next interaction box? Query Tom. Tom, I interrupted. I've noticed that you usually go on to something new just when we come to a really good idea that I'd like to spend more time with. You're exactly right. Not at Tom. It's important when generating ideas to really generate ideas. You want to keep moving. It's just as dangerous to get stuck on a good idea as on a bad one. There'll always be plenty of time for expanding on any idea later on. And let's move, I said eagerly. The next interaction box is one I call add something to the interaction. One way you can do this is by adding an intermediary of some kind. A technical example would be how for centuries people applied paint with brushes. A labor intensive process, as you know. Then one day someone thought to add an intermediary to the process. Air and spray paint was born. Another way to add something to an interaction is by adding an element of feedback. Add a small round hole and the kettle whistles when the water starts to boil. And how many lives have been saved because someone thought to design smoke detectors that beep at you when the battery gets low. Now Linda, start supplementing the interaction. Linda launched right in. Intermediary. Well, we could ask a local celebrity to appear in the ad. A well-known author who might be willing to say something about why people should come to our sale. I know plenty of local authors who would be willing to do that, I said nodding along. They sure would want to see this bookstore succeed. Good, said Tom. Now feedback. What if we put a coupon in the ad? Suggested Linda. That asked people to vote on what books they would like to see go on sale. Not only would that be fun, but it would give us valuable feedback on the books people are really interested in. It would also be a good way to get people involved or to intensify the interaction as you say. You're really getting the hang of this Linda, said Tom. I assume your boss has told you about the boxes we've talked about so far and the importance of coming up with a lot of ideas quickly. He has. Good. Then why don't you two come up with some more ideas as quickly as you can as I take you through some of the boxes I like to use for marketing. I looked to Linda, who was already nodding. I smiled and nodded along too. Great, said Tom. Are you both ready? Ready, we said in unison. Add a new dimension, began Tom. Perhaps there's some aspect of the sale you can highlight or a different way of looking at it. What about the dimension of quantity offered Linda? We could highlight the number of books going on sale. 2,207 books that will never be priced this low again. I jumped in. Or we could highlight the savings, 20% to 50% off every book in the store. Then there's the time dimension added Linda. Why this Sunday will be the best day in years to get the books you want. And I added since a new dimension could also be a different way of looking at it instead of calling it a book sale, we could call it a build your library for less sale. Or said Linda, we could add a new dimension by relating it to something else, such as saying it's the next best thing to having an uncle in the book business. Gosh, this is fun. She chuckled. Great, now divide it up, said Tom, moving us right along. Linda again. Instead of featuring just a single book, we could divide the ad into a grid and show many different books with their prices. Bring some things together, said Tom, quickening the pace. This is wide open. What can you combine with the headline or bring into the ad that would make it more interesting or attention getting? I suggested we could tap into something in the news. Want to understand what's going on between Ukraine and Russia? Now you can. For less. Do it beforehand. We could send out postcards, suggested Linda, or make phone calls. Telling people to look for the big ad in the paper this Sunday. Or even inviting them personally. Better yet, I added, what if before the main sale, we had a special sale for our regulars? And for the grand finale, announced Tom, do the opposite. The opposite. Mused Linda, biting her lip. What could that be? Let's see. The opposite of selling books is giving them away? This one's making me nervous, I joked. So she continued, how do we make it work that we give books away? What if instead of a 20% discount, we just gave away every fifth book for free? That's mathematically the same, isn't it? Well, yes, I agreed, shrugging. If the book doesn't cost any more than the average price of the other four books. We can make some kind of stipulation, said Linda. But the important thing is that it would enable us to advertise that we're giving away free books. And people might be tempted to buy more books than they otherwise would in order to get that fifth book without having to pay for it. They just might, I agreed. Linda jumped up suddenly. My brain can't handle any more ideas, she exclaimed. I'm going to take the best of all of this and write an ad that's going to raise a lot of eyebrows. And she turned on her heel and practically skipped away.
